basic_info.name: CO2 Absorbent Selection
basic_info.description: HSP distance between CO2 and candidate absorbent solvents predicts absorption capacity for carbon capture applications. By comparing the HSP of CO2 under process conditions with the HSP of physical absorbents (ionic liquids, deep eutectic solvents, organic solvents, amine solutions), rapid screening of absorbent candidates is enabled. This HSP-based approach complements traditional thermodynamic models (equation of state, activity coefficient models) by providing an intuitive framework for initial solvent selection in carbon capture and utilization (CCU) processes.

hsp_principle.hsp_principle: CO2 absorption in physical solvents follows the 'like dissolves like' principle: solvents whose HSP closely match those of CO2 exhibit higher physical absorption capacities (Henry's law constants). CO2 has distinct HSP values (dD ~15.6, dP ~5.2, dH ~5.8 MPa^0.5 at standard conditions) with moderate dispersion, low-moderate polarity, and moderate hydrogen bonding due to its quadrupole moment and Lewis acid character. The Ra distance between CO2 HSP and absorbent HSP correlates inversely with physical CO2 solubility. For chemical absorbents (amines, carbonate solutions), HSP provides partial guidance but the dominant absorption mechanism is chemical reaction (carbamate/bicarbonate formation), which is not directly captured by HSP. However, even for chemical absorbents, the physical solubility component (governed by HSP matching) affects the overall absorption rate by determining CO2 availability at the reaction site. For ionic liquids and deep eutectic solvents as novel CO2 absorbents, HSP screening is particularly valuable because their tunable structure allows systematic HSP optimization.
hsp_principle.key_equation: Ra = sqrt(4*(dD_CO2-dD_absorbent)^2 + (dP_CO2-dP_absorbent)^2 + (dH_CO2-dH_absorbent)^2); Henry's constant H inversely correlates with 1/Ra^2; Physical solubility ~ exp(-V_CO2 * Ra^2 / (6RT))
hsp_principle.typical_red_threshold: Ra < 5 MPa^0.5 for high physical absorption capacity; Ra < 8 MPa^0.5 for moderate absorption [uncertain]

required_inputs: CO2 HSP at process temperature and pressure (dD, dP, dH)
Absorbent HSP (dD, dP, dH) for each candidate
CO2 partial pressure in feed gas
data_sources: HSPiP database for common organic solvents
Published CO2 HSP values (temperature and pressure dependent)
Published ionic liquid HSP from group contribution or COSMO-RS
Experimental Henry's law constants for validation
COSMO-RS calculations for novel absorbent HSP estimation
complementary_parameters: CO2 partial pressure and total pressure
Absorbent thermal stability and degradation temperature
Heat of absorption (energy cost for regeneration)
Chemical reactivity with CO2 (for chemical absorbents)

limitations: CO2 HSP are strongly temperature and pressure dependent, requiring T/P corrections that introduce uncertainty
Chemical absorption (amine-CO2 reactions) dominates in many practical systems and is not captured by HSP distance
HSP treats CO2 as a simple molecule, ignoring its Lewis acid character and specific interactions with basic solvents
Ionic liquids have complex solvation mechanisms (cavity formation, specific anion-CO2 interactions) beyond simple HSP matching
Multi-component gas mixtures (flue gas containing N2, O2, H2O, SOx, NOx) have selectivity requirements not addressed by CO2 HSP alone
Absorption kinetics (mass transfer coefficients, interfacial area) are equally important as thermodynamic capacity but not predicted by HSP
known_failure_modes: Chemical absorbents (MEA, DEA, MDEA) where chemical reaction enthalpy dominates and HSP distance poorly predicts capacity
Functionalized ionic liquids (task-specific ILs with amine groups) where chemical and physical absorption coexist
High-pressure CO2 systems (supercritical conditions) where CO2 HSP change dramatically and standard correlations fail
Water-lean solvents where the absence of water fundamentally changes the absorption mechanism compared to aqueous systems
validation_method: Gravimetric or volumetric CO2 absorption measurement
Henry's law constant determination via gas-liquid equilibrium
Vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) measurements for CO2-solvent systems
CO2 loading measurement (mol CO2/mol absorbent) at various T and P
Absorption rate measurement in wetted-wall column or stirred cell
Correlation analysis: Ra vs. measured Henry's constant across solvent series

key_references.author: Venkatraman, V.; Evjen, S.; Lethesh, K.C.; Raj, J.J.; Knuutila, H.K.; Fiksdahl, A.
key_references.title: Rapid, comprehensive screening of ionic liquids towards sustainable applications

key_references.author: Moganty, S.S.; Baltus, R.E.
key_references.title: Regular solution theory for low pressure carbon dioxide solubility in room temperature ionic liquids: ionic liquid solubility parameter from activation energy of viscosity

key_references.author: Hansen, C.M.
key_references.title: Hansen Solubility Parameters: A User's Handbook, 2nd Edition

key_references.author: Williams, L.L.; Rubin, J.B.; Edwards, H.W.
key_references.title: Calculation of Hansen solubility parameter values for a range of pressure and temperature conditions, including the supercritical fluid region

literature.regulatory_relevance: HSP-based absorbent screening supports CCS/CCU regulatory frameworks including EU ETS (Emissions Trading System) compliance, EPA Clean Air Act greenhouse gas regulations, and Paris Agreement NDC targets. REACH compliance required for novel absorbent solvents used in industrial carbon capture. IEA and IPCC guidelines for CCS technology assessment benefit from rapid solvent screening methods.

industry_sectors: Oil and gas (natural gas sweetening, enhanced oil recovery)
commercial_tools: Aspen Plus (process simulation with thermodynamic models)
